structureHistory.buildEra: Pre–Civil War (antebellum; before 1861)
structureHistory.originalUse: Private residence
structureHistory.currentUse: Office building (adaptive reuse)
structureHistory.styleNotes: Brick dwelling later painted; basic massing and historic features retained through change of use, per Beaver Life Magazine’s local HARB / Secretary of the Interior Standards example write-up. Exact architect and construction year are not stated in that source.
alterations: Function changed from residence to office
Brick painted (surface treatment noted in local preservation article)

structureHistory.districtFabric: Third Street’s commercial core is generally characterized by two- and three-story mid-19th through early-20th-century buildings built to the sidewalk with little or no front setback; Italianate commercial fronts are common elsewhere on the corridor. 1147 is notable in local writing as a former house within that CBD setting rather than a purpose-built storefront block.

landLotPlat.summary: The building sits on Beaver’s planned 1792 town plat, surveyed by Deputy Surveyor Daniel Leet under a 1791 Pennsylvania Act authorizing the community. The original grid used 100-foot major rights-of-way and 25-foot secondary streets, with eight reserved public squares.
landLotPlat.lotTypes: Original plan distinguished interior “in-lots” (rectangular building lots within 300′ × 360′ squares) and larger eastern “out-lots.” Later 19th–early 20th-century subdivision reduced many parcels.
landLotPlat.thirdStreetRole: Third Street is the borough’s primary commercial and transportation corridor (today PA 68), unusually wide in the CBD and lined with historic commercial fabric. NRHP materials place Third Street at the core of the Beaver Historic District.
publicSquaresNearby: Agnew Square (formerly Gibson) — Beaver County Courthouse at Third & Market

Corner squares named for Clark, Bouquet, Wayne, and Brodhead/Linn honor regional military figures
cbdEvolution: 1837 directories show a small service-center mix (merchants, trades, innkeepers) on Third Street
By 1876, lawyers and physicians lined Third Street as industrial valley towns grew elsewhere
By 1921, auto garages, gas stations, restaurants, and professional offices had largely replaced older craft trades
1924 tornado damaged the Third & Market area (documented in local photographic history)

notableContextOccupantsOnCorridor: Historic Third Street housed merchants, attorneys, physicians, banks, and later auto-related businesses — typical county-seat CBD succession rather than a single industrial employer row.
County government and courthouse functions concentrated near Third & Market, reinforcing professional office demand along the spine.

indigenousPeoples.summary: Before European-American permanent settlement, the Ohio–Beaver confluence was a significant Indigenous meeting and habitation landscape in the Ohio Country — not a blank wilderness. This section describes regional peoples and places; it does not invent a private deed claim tying 1147 Third Street to a specific Indigenous owner.

peoples.name: Shawnee
peoples.notes: Present in the broader Ohio Country; Wikipedia and regional histories note Shawnee presence around the Beaver area before later displacement pressures of the mid-18th century wars and diplomacy.

peoples.name: Lenape (Delaware)
peoples.notes: Major presence in western Pennsylvania and the Ohio Country after eastward dispossession; parties to the Treaty of Fort McIntosh (1785). Earlier French-allied log towns at the confluence included Delaware (Lenape) communities.

peoples.name: Mingoes (Ohio Iroquoians / Seneca-affiliated communities)
peoples.notes: Mingo bands are repeatedly named among peoples gathering at the confluence villages in mid-18th-century accounts.

peoples.name: Wyandot, Ojibwe (Chippewa), Odawa (Ottawa)
peoples.notes: Signatory nations at the Treaty of Fort McIntosh (1785), which imposed cessions covering large parts of the Ohio Country and cleared Pennsylvania title claims for Depreciation Lands surveys.

placesAndNames: Sawcunk / Beaver’s Town / Shingas’ Old Town — Indigenous settlement names associated with the confluence zone (c. 1730s–1780s meeting landscape)
SAH Archipedia notes French-allied square-log houses with stone chimneys here in the 1750s; Native habitation in the immediate area is described as ending after the 1763 Bushy Run campaign context
indigenousPeoples.ethicalNote: Treaties at Fort Stanwix (1784) and Fort McIntosh (1785) were coercive instruments of U.S. and Pennsylvania land acquisition. Many Ohio Country nations, including Shawnee, rejected Fort McIntosh’s legitimacy — feeding the Western Confederacy and Northwest Indian War (to Greenville, 1795).

fortsAndWars.summary: Beaver’s river bluff is one of the most strategically charged Revolutionary–early Federal landscapes in western Pennsylvania. Fort McIntosh (1778) stood south of today’s Third Street CBD on the Ohio River bank; its archaeological site is both individually listed (NRHP 1975) and a key contributing resource of the Beaver Historic District.
frenchAndIndianWar: The Ohio Country — including the Beaver–Ohio confluence — was contested among French, British, and Indigenous nations.

Col. Henry Bouquet’s campaigns (including the 1763 Bushy Run victory) are commemorated in local square naming and regional military memory; they mark the collapse of sustained Indigenous village life at the confluence after Pontiac’s War-era fighting.
revolutionaryWarFrontier: Fort McIntosh built 1778 under Gen. Lachlan McIntosh; designed with French engineer Cambray-Digny; first U.S. fort north of the Ohio River.
Trapezoidal log fort with bastions, ditch, barracks, and river access — base for western Continental operations toward Detroit / Ohio Country.
Treaty of Fort McIntosh signed 21 January 1785 with Wyandot, Delaware, Chippewa, and Ottawa representatives — establishing early Northwest survey baselines (Seven Ranges) from this vicinity.
Fort declined as the frontier moved west; abandoned by the early 1790s; archaeological excavation 1970s recovered extensive material culture; memorialized today along River Road.
nearby: Legionville (nearby) — winter quarters of Gen. Anthony Wayne’s Legion of the United States, 1792–93
Fort Pitt (Pittsburgh) — upstream command node for the western theater

historicDistrict.name: Beaver Historic District
historicDistrict.periodOfSignificance: Primarily mid-19th century through mid-20th century building stock; community plan roots in 1792
historicDistrict.resources: NR materials cite ~1,456 resources; ~1,258 contributing; Fort McIntosh site called out among the most significant contributing properties
historicDistrict.boundariesNote: Roughly bounded by P&LE Railroad tracks, Fair Ave., 5th St., 3rd St., and Sassafras Ln. (Wikipedia / NR summary); encompasses most of the borough and the entire 1792 plat core
historicDistrict.phmcMarker: Pennsylvania Historical & Museum Commission marker designation noted for the district (1946 context in secondary summaries)
historicDistrict.localOrdinance: Beaver administers a local historic district ordinance (Historic District Act / Act 167) requiring certificates of appropriateness for demolitions within boundaries coterminous with the NR district
historicDistrict.relevanceToProperty: 1147 Third Street lies on the commercial Third Street spine that the NRHP nomination treats as the district’s core. Local HARB guidance cites this exact address as a Preservation-standard adaptive reuse example.

timeline.event: Indigenous meeting towns at the Ohio–Beaver confluence (Sawcunk / Beaver’s Town / Shingas’ Old Town); Shawnee, Lenape, Mingo presence in the Ohio Country.
French & Indian War / Pontiac’s War reshape control of the Ohio Country; post–Bushy Run era ends sustained Native habitation at the confluence per regional architectural histories.
Fort McIntosh constructed on the Ohio bluff at present-day Beaver.
Treaty of Fort McIntosh; Depreciation Lands surveying follows for Revolutionary War veterans’ certificates.
Pennsylvania Act authorizes Beaver; Daniel Leet surveys the grid-and-squares town plan.
Beaver becomes county seat; first county court established.
1147 Third Street built as a private residence (local preservation source: pre–Civil War).
Peak construction era for most Beaver Historic District buildings; Third Street evolves from craft service center to professional / auto-era CBD.
Pittsburgh & Lake Erie Railroad accelerates residential growth; Beaver remains largely non-industrial county seat.
Tornado damages Third & Market commercial area.
Fort McIntosh site listed on the National Register.
Beaver Historic District listed on the National Register (Oct 24).
1147 Third Street occupied as painted-brick office (Town Center Associates cited); held up locally as a Preservation example under Secretary of the Interior Standards.

parcelIdentity.summary: County government seat on Agnew Square (formerly Gibson Square) at Third Street and Market Street — the civic heart of Beaver’s Third Street historic commercial district. The present 2002–2003 courthouse replaces earlier buildings on the same reserved public square, including the 1875/1877 Renaissance-style courthouse damaged by fire in 1932 and later demolished.

parcelIdentity.historicDistrict: Beaver Historic District (NRHP #96001201) — courthouse site / park counted among district civic resources

knownOccupants.name: Beaver County Court of Common Pleas (36th Judicial District)

caveats: The present building is modern (2003) and is documented here primarily for continuous civic use of the Agnew Square site and its role in the Third Street CBD / NR district narrative.
Saved Art Deco / concrete reliefs from earlier fabric are displayed in interior courtyards of the new building per secondary architectural summaries.

structureHistory.buildEra: Present building 2002–2003 (site occupied by successive courthouses since early 1800s; major 1875 generation; fire 1932; razed for replacement)
structureHistory.originalUse: County courthouse / public square civic complex
structureHistory.currentUse: Beaver County Courthouse
structureHistory.styleNotes: Present building: two-story buff stone, concrete, and glass with projected wings, glass-enclosed atrium/tower with clock, and a curved pavilion connecting the wings — described in county-seat architectural surveys as a modern composition with Art Moderne echoes. Earlier 1875 Renaissance-style courthouse by Thomas Boyd was renovated after the 1932 fire (Edward J. Carlisle / Harry P. Sharrer) and later demolished. A nearly exact replica Victorian clock tower (dedicated 2002) commemorates the 1877 tower cupola nearby in the CBD streetscape.
alterations: 1803 and 1810 early courthouse generations on the county-seat site
1875/1877 Boyd courthouse; 1932 fire; 1933 renovation; 1974 addition
2002–2003 demolition and replacement by current courthouse
2002 Victorian clock-tower replica and CBD streetscape restoration nearby (Heritage Foundation advisory role)
structureHistory.districtFabric: NR district materials call out five churches and the county courthouse among the most prominent non-residential buildings, even though most district resources are residential. Agnew Square and McIntosh Square (Soldiers and Sailors Monument, 1900) form the ceremonial civic pair across Third Street.

Third Street CBD streetscape north of the College Avenue institutional belt

parcelIdentity.summary: Gray rock-faced sandstone Richardsonian Romanesque church at College Avenue and Corporation Street — one of five historic churches called out as prominent landmarks in Beaver Historic District literature. Built 1890–92 to designs by regional architect James P. Bailey; Wikipedia’s district article uses this building as its lead illustration.
parcelIdentity.cbdCorridor: College Avenue (institutional belt adjacent to Third Street CBD)
parcelIdentity.historicDistrict: Beaver Historic District (NRHP #96001201) — contributing ecclesiastical landmark
parcelIdentity.localHarb: Subject to Beaver Borough HARB / Act 167 local historic district review
caveats: NR district inventory resource numbers in secondary adaptations (e.g. #979) are cited from nomination materials; verify against the NARA registration form for formal citations.
Do not treat Google Maps or private listing-site parcel scrapes as sources.

structureHistory.originalUse: Presbyterian church
structureHistory.currentUse: Presbyterian church (with later educational wing)
structureHistory.styleNotes: Richardsonian Romanesque: intersecting gable roof system, prominent square corner tower dominating the local skyline, gray rock-faced sandstone with foliated and geometric stone carving. SAH Archipedia notes Bailey’s debt to H. H. Richardson’s Allegheny County Courthouse vocabulary (arches, tower rhythm, carved capitals). A large educational wing north of the church is executed in compatible stone.
alterations: Educational wing added north of the sanctuary in a compatible stone style (date varies by secondary source; fabric remains complementary)
Ongoing maintenance typical of a continuously used sanctuary within a local HARB district
structureHistory.districtFabric: NR / Living Places materials describe Beaver’s historic churches as spanning roughly forty years of ecclesiastical design; First Presbyterian (1890–92) is called the earliest of that prominent set, followed by other College/Commerce corridor churches in Late Gothic and Romanesque Revival idioms.

landLotPlat.summary: The property sits on Beaver’s planned 1792 town plat, surveyed by Deputy Surveyor Daniel Leet under a 1791 Pennsylvania Act authorizing the community. The original grid used 100-foot major rights-of-way and 25-foot secondary streets, with eight reserved public squares.
landLotPlat.lotTypes: Original plan distinguished interior “in-lots” (rectangular building lots within 300′ × 360′ squares) and larger eastern “out-lots.” Later 19th–early 20th-century subdivision reduced many parcels.
publicSquaresNearby: Agnew Square (formerly Gibson) — Beaver County Courthouse at Third & Market
McIntosh Square — Soldiers and Sailors Monument (1900)
Corner squares named for Clark, Bouquet, Wayne, and Brodhead/Linn honor regional military figures
cbdEvolution: 1837 directories show a small service-center mix (merchants, trades, innkeepers) on Third Street
By 1876, lawyers and physicians lined Third Street as industrial valley towns grew elsewhere
By 1921, auto garages, gas stations, restaurants, and professional offices had largely replaced older craft trades
1924 tornado damaged the Third & Market area (documented in local photographic history)
landLotPlat.thirdStreetRole: College Avenue and Corporation Street sit in the institutional belt immediately tied to the Third Street commercial spine. The church tower is a skyline marker for visitors approaching the CBD and for the residential neighborhoods that make up most of the NR district’s contributing resources.

earlyOwnersOccupants.summary: Public architectural histories emphasize Bailey’s design and the congregation’s late-19th-century building campaign rather than a deep private deed narrative. The building has remained in ecclesiastical use, which simplifies occupancy history relative to commercial adaptive-reuse properties on Third Street.
notableContextOccupantsOnCorridor: James P. Bailey also designed Old Main at Geneva College (Beaver Falls) and the 1886 Butler County Courthouse — placing this church in a regional late-Victorian practice.
NR district writings group First Presbyterian with Park United Presbyterian, First Christian, United Methodist, and SS. Peter and Paul as the five prominent historic churches.
researchGaps: Congregation building-committee minutes and contractor contracts for 1890–92
Exact educational-wing construction date from primary records
Sanborn map footprint growth over the 20th century

indigenousPeoples.summary: Before European-American permanent settlement, the Ohio–Beaver confluence was a significant Indigenous meeting and habitation landscape in the Ohio Country — not a blank wilderness. This section describes regional peoples and places; it does not invent a private deed claim tying this address to a specific Indigenous owner.

peoples.name: Shawnee
peoples.notes: Present in the broader Ohio Country; regional histories note Shawnee presence around the Beaver area before mid-18th century displacement pressures.

peoples.name: Lenape (Delaware)
peoples.notes: Major presence in western Pennsylvania and the Ohio Country after eastward dispossession; parties to the Treaty of Fort McIntosh (1785).

peoples.name: Mingoes (Ohio Iroquoians / Seneca-affiliated communities)
peoples.notes: Mingo bands are repeatedly named among peoples gathering at the confluence villages in mid-18th-century accounts.

peoples.name: Wyandot, Ojibwe (Chippewa), Odawa (Ottawa)
peoples.notes: Signatory nations at the Treaty of Fort McIntosh (1785), which imposed cessions covering large parts of the Ohio Country.

placesAndNames: Sawcunk / Beaver’s Town / Shingas’ Old Town — Indigenous settlement names associated with the confluence zone
SAH Archipedia notes French-allied square-log houses with stone chimneys here in the 1750s; Native habitation in the immediate area is described as ending after the 1763 Bushy Run campaign context
indigenousPeoples.ethicalNote: Treaties at Fort Stanwix (1784) and Fort McIntosh (1785) were coercive instruments of U.S. and Pennsylvania land acquisition. Many Ohio Country nations rejected Fort McIntosh’s legitimacy — feeding the Western Confederacy and Northwest Indian War (to Greenville, 1795).

fortsAndWars.summary: Beaver’s river bluff is one of the most strategically charged Revolutionary–early Federal landscapes in western Pennsylvania. Fort McIntosh (1778) stood south of today’s Third Street CBD on the Ohio River bank; its archaeological site is both individually listed (NRHP 1975) and a key contributing resource of the Beaver Historic District.
frenchAndIndianWar: The Ohio Country — including the Beaver–Ohio confluence — was contested among French, British, and Indigenous nations.
Col. Henry Bouquet’s campaigns (including the 1763 Bushy Run victory) are commemorated in local square naming and regional military memory.
revolutionaryWarFrontier: Fort McIntosh built 1778 under Gen. Lachlan McIntosh; designed with French engineer Cambray-Digny; first U.S. fort north of the Ohio River.
Trapezoidal log fort with bastions, ditch, barracks, and river access — base for western Continental operations.
Treaty of Fort McIntosh signed 21 January 1785 with Wyandot, Delaware, Chippewa, and Ottawa representatives.
Fort declined as the frontier moved west; abandoned by the early 1790s; archaeological excavation 1970s recovered extensive material culture.
nearby: Legionville (nearby) — winter quarters of Gen. Anthony Wayne’s Legion of the United States, 1792–93
Fort Pitt (Pittsburgh) — upstream command node for the western theater

historicDistrict.resources: NR materials cite ~1,456 resources; ~1,258 contributing; Fort McIntosh site and Quay House called out among the most significant contributing properties
historicDistrict.boundariesNote: Roughly bounded by P&LE Railroad tracks, Fair Ave., 5th St., 3rd St., and Sassafras Ln.; encompasses most of the borough and the entire 1792 plat core
historicDistrict.phmcMarker: Pennsylvania Historical & Museum Commission marker designation noted for the district (1946 context in secondary summaries)
historicDistrict.localOrdinance: Beaver administers a local historic district ordinance (Historic District Act / Act 167) requiring certificates of appropriateness for demolitions within boundaries coterminous with the NR district
historicDistrict.relevanceToProperty: First Presbyterian is the lead photograph on Wikipedia’s Beaver Historic District page and is explicitly named in NR-adapted descriptions as the earliest of the district’s five prominent historic churches — a skyline-defining Richardsonian Romanesque landmark on the College Avenue institutional edge of the Third Street CBD.

timeline.event: Leet surveys Beaver’s grid; College Avenue corridor takes shape within the reserved town plan.
James P. Bailey practices architecture in the region (Geneva College Old Main; Butler County Courthouse).
First Presbyterian Church built in Richardsonian Romanesque sandstone with corner tower.
Companion historic churches rise nearby (First Christian; Park United Presbyterian; Methodist Episcopal), defining the ecclesiastical skyline set.
Beaver Historic District listed; church counted among prominent contributing ecclesiastical landmarks.
Widely reused public-domain exterior photograph by Nyttend documents the College Avenue elevation.

structureHistory.buildEra: 1778 (abandoned early 1790s; archaeological recovery 1970s)
structureHistory.originalUse: U.S. frontier fort (first north of the Ohio River)
structureHistory.currentUse: Memorialized archaeological park / heritage site
structureHistory.styleNotes: Trapezoidal log fort with bastions, ditch, barracks, warehouses, officers’ quarters, forge, kitchen, and powder magazines; ~150 feet per side; river-side slope defense; underground supply passage to the Ohio. Extant features today are stone footers, granite/sandstone memorials, and bronze plaques.
alterations: Abandonment and decay by the 1790s (roofs gone by 1795 per secondary accounts)
1974–78 University of Pittsburgh / Carnegie Museum excavations (~80,000 identifiable artifacts)
1978 bicentennial dedication; later granite/sandstone memorial donated c. 2010
structureHistory.districtFabric: The fort site is the Revolutionary-era substrate beneath Beaver’s later county-seat grid; McIntosh Square in the 1792 plat commemorates the fort by name.

landLotPlat.summary: The fort predates the 1792 Leet plat. When Beaver was laid out, the river bluff fort site remained a distinct military/heritage landscape south of the commercial Third Street grid and public squares (including McIntosh Square).

landLotPlat.thirdStreetRole: Third Street developed upslope as the civilian commercial spine after the fort’s military purpose faded; the fort site remains the foundational frontier landmark for the borough’s origin story.

earlyOwnersOccupants.summary: Military occupation under Continental / early U.S. Army command rather than private deed ownership during the fort’s active years. notableContextOccupantsOnCorridor: Gen. Lachlan McIntosh moved ~1,500 men from Pittsburgh to Beaver after fort completion (fall 1778).

parcelIdentity.summary: Aliquippa station is a former railway station located in Aliquippa, Pennsylvania, United States. The station was constructed and used by the now defunct Pittsburgh and Lake Erie Railroad (P&LE). Constructed in 1911, the station has also gone by the name of Woodlawn station because of the former town of Woodlawn that was merged with Aliquippa in the late 1870s. After the station closed to passengers, it was used for several years by the Jones and Laughlin Steel Company as an office building. The structure currently sits vacant just outside the city of Aliquippa. properties.name: B.F. Jones Memorial Library
properties.subtitle: United States historic place — public-source hybrid dossier

parcelIdentity.summary: The B.F. Jones Memorial Library is a historic library in Aliquippa, a city in Beaver County, Pennsylvania, United States. Named for steel tycoon Benjamin Franklin Jones, it was built in 1927 with money donated by Jones' daughter. As a product of the profits of the Jones and Laughlin Steel Company, for which Aliquippa was a company town, it resembled a Carnegie library. Rated a high-quality example of small-scale Renaissance Revival architecture, the two-story library is built primarily of Indiana Limestone with a bronze roof. Among its leading features are multiple plaster casts, windows of stained glass, carvings in imitation of those made by Italian sculptor Andrea della Robbia, and a large statue of Jones.…

properties.subtitle: United States historic place — public-source hybrid dossier

parcelIdentity.summary: Legionville (or Legion Ville) was the first formal basic training facility for the military of the United States. The camp, which was established in winter 1792 under the command of Major General Anthony Wayne, was near present-day Baden, Pennsylvania, a suburb of Pittsburgh. It was used to train the soldiers which would become the Legion of the United States. Throughout the winter of 1792-93, existing troops along with new recruits were drilled in military skills, tactics and discipline. properties.name: Beginning Point of the U.S. Public Land Survey
properties.subtitle: Site in Ohio and Pennsylvania, US — public-source hybrid dossier

heroImage.title: Beginning Point of the U.S. Public Land Survey

images.title: Beginning Point of the U.S. Public Land Survey

address.street: Survey marker site (OH/PA line near East Liverpool)

parcelIdentity.summary: The Beginning Point of the U.S. Public Land Survey is the point from which the United States in 1786 began the formal survey of the lands known then as the Northwest Territory, now making up all of Ohio, Indiana, Illinois, Michigan, Wisconsin, and part of Minnesota. The survey is claimed to be the first major cadastral survey undertaken by any nation. The point now lies underwater on the state line between Ohio and Pennsylvania. Because it is submerged, a monument commemorating the point is adjacent to the nearest roadway and located on the state line between East Liverpool, Ohio and Ohioville, Pennsylvania. The area around the marker was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1965.

parcelIdentity.summary: The Watts Mill Bridge is a pin-connected Pratt pony truss bridge located over the Little Beaver Creek in Cannelton, Pennsylvania, United States. The bridge and adjacent Mill remains were listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1988. parcelIdentity.summary: The Davis Island Lock and Dam Site on the Ohio River in Avalon, Pennsylvania, is the site of the former Davis Island lock that was completed in 1885. The lock and dam existed from 1878 to 1922, designed by William Emery Merrill and the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers. The Davis Island Lock and Dam was the first dam that was constructed on the Ohio River. It officially opened on October 7, 1885, with a large dedication ceremony. The Davis Island Dam was the largest Chanoine dam built in the 19th century, and one of the first concrete structures built by the Army Corps of Engineers. It was the first of 51 Chanoine type dams that were built by the Corps of Engineers between 1878 and 1929. The dam was dismantled in…

parcelIdentity.summary: The Andrew Carnegie Free Library & Music Hall (also known as the ACFL&MH or "Carnegie Carnegie") is a public library and music hall located at 300 Beechwood Avenue in Carnegie, Pennsylvania. Like hundreds of other Carnegie libraries, the construction of the ACFL&MH, which opened in 1901, was funded by industrialist Andrew Carnegie. The ACFL&MH has been recognized as a historic landmark and appears on the List of Pittsburgh History and Landmarks Foundation Historic Landmarks and the National Register of Historic Places.

properties.name: Woodville
properties.subtitle: Historic house in Pennsylvania, United States — public-source hybrid dossier

parcelIdentity.summary: Woodville, also known as the Neville House or John Neville House, is a house which is located on Washington Pike (PA 50) south of Heidelberg, Pennsylvania, United States. It is significant for its association with John Neville, a tax collector whose other house was burned in the Whiskey Rebellion in 1794. The oldest portion of the house dates to 1775, with a main section built a decade later. It is one of the oldest houses in Allegheny County, preserved and restored to its original condition. For those reasons, it was declared a National Historic Landmark in 1983. After being significantly renovated by an early 19th-century resident, it remained a private house until 1975. Today it is a historic house museum.
